title: Modern CSS - Container Queries, Subgrid, clamp(), :has() Selector

seo.title: Modern CSS 2026 - Container Queries, Subgrid, clamp, :has, aspect-ratio | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master the latest CSS features: @container queries, subgrid, clamp() fluid typography, aspect-ratio, :has() parent selector, color-mix(), logical properties, scroll-snap, and text-wrap: balance. 56+ practice questions.
seo.keywords: container queries css, subgrid css, clamp css, aspect-ratio css, :has css selector, color-mix css, logical properties css, scroll-snap css, text-wrap balance, modern css 2026, accent-color css

content.what:
What Is Modern CSS?

In the last 3 years, CSS has had the biggest wave of new features since Grid and Flexbox. Problems that used to require JavaScript, media queries, or clever hacks can now be solved with one CSS line. This chapter is a guided tour of the most important features that shipped in every major browser between 2022 and 2026.

You will learn:

  • Container queries - style an element based on its container's size, not the viewport
  • Subgrid - let nested grid items align to the parent grid's tracks
  • clamp() - fluid values that scale between a min and max
  • aspect-ratio - lock an element's width-to-height ratio (no more padding hacks)
  • :has() - the parent selector (finally!)
  • Logical properties - margin-inline, padding-block for RTL-friendly code
  • accent-color - theme form controls with one property
  • color-mix() - blend two colors in CSS
  • :focus-visible - focus rings only for keyboard users
  • scroll-snap - carousels and galleries without JavaScript
  • text-wrap: balance - beautifully wrapped headings

These are not experimental - they work in every browser you need to support. If you are building a new site in 2026, use them.
content.why:
Why Learn Modern CSS?

1. Less JavaScript, Smaller Bundles

Container queries replace resize-observer code. :has() replaces 'add class on click' logic. scroll-snap replaces carousel libraries. Every feature in this chapter removes code you would otherwise have to write and maintain in JavaScript.

2. Truly Component-Based Responsive Design

Media queries respond to the viewport — but a card in a sidebar does not know about the viewport. It knows about its sidebar. Container queries let components respond to THEIR container, so the same card looks right whether it is in a wide hero or a narrow column. This is the missing piece for component-driven design systems.

3. Fluid Interfaces with Zero Breakpoints

clamp(min, preferred, max) gives you fluid typography and spacing that scales smoothly from mobile to desktop without any breakpoints. Aarav's whole site uses font-size: clamp(16px, 2vw, 24px) and looks perfect at every size.

4. Better Accessibility Out of the Box

:focus-visible gets focus rings right without JavaScript. Logical properties work correctly in right-to-left languages like Arabic and Hebrew. aspect-ratio prevents layout shift when images load. Modern CSS bakes accessibility into the platform.

5. Beautiful Typography for Free

text-wrap: balance automatically rebalances a heading so it does not end with a single orphan word. text-wrap: pretty improves long paragraphs. One property, beautiful output, no manual line breaks.
content.explanation:
Deep Dive into Modern CSS Features

1. Container Queries

Container queries let you style an element based on its container's size rather than the viewport. First, mark an element as a container with container-type, then use @container to write queries.

.sidebar {
  container-type: inline-size;
  container-name: sidebar;
}

.card {
  display: block;
}

@container sidebar (min-width: 400px) {
  .card {
    display: grid;
    grid-template-columns: 120px 1fr;
    gap: 16px;
  }
}

When the .sidebar becomes at least 400px wide, the card inside it switches from stacked to a horizontal layout. The viewport is irrelevant — it is the container that matters.

container-type values:

  • inline-size - queries based on width (most common)
  • size - queries based on both width and height
  • normal - not a container (default)

2. Subgrid

Normally, nested grids have their own independent tracks. grid-template-columns: subgrid makes the child grid use the parent's tracks instead. This solves the classic problem of aligning items across cards.

.cards {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);
  gap: 20px;
}

.card {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-rows: subgrid; /* inherit parent rows */
  grid-row: span 3;
}
/* Every card's title, body, and footer line up perfectly
   across the whole row, even if content lengths differ. */

3. clamp() for Fluid Values

clamp(min, preferred, max) returns the preferred value, but never less than min or more than max. It is perfect for fluid typography:

h1 {
  /* Between 28px and 56px, scaling with viewport */
  font-size: clamp(28px, 5vw, 56px);
}

.container {
  /* Padding that grows with the screen */
  padding: clamp(16px, 5%, 48px);
}

On a 400px-wide phone, 5vw = 20px, so the font is 28px (clamped to min). On a 1200px-wide desktop, 5vw = 60px, so the font is 56px (clamped to max). In between, it scales smoothly.

4. aspect-ratio

Locking the width-to-height ratio of an element used to require the padding-top hack. Now it is one property:

.video-wrapper {
  aspect-ratio: 16 / 9;
  width: 100%;
}

.avatar {
  aspect-ratio: 1;  /* square */
  width: 60px;
}

No more layout shift when images load. Set width or height and the other dimension follows automatically.

5. The :has() Parent Selector

CSS finally has a parent selector. :has() matches an element based on its descendants.

/* Style a card that contains an image */
.card:has(img) {
  padding: 0;
}

/* Turn a form red if any input is invalid */
form:has(input:invalid) {
  border-left: 4px solid red;
}

/* Highlight a label that contains a checked checkbox */
label:has(:checked) {
  background: #a855f7;
  color: white;
}

/* Dim the body when a modal is open */
body:has(.modal[open]) {
  overflow: hidden;
}

Hundreds of patterns that used to require JavaScript are now one CSS rule.

6. Logical Properties

CSS had margin-left and margin-right — but what is 'left' in Arabic, which reads right-to-left? Logical properties use 'inline' (the reading direction) and 'block' (the flow direction) instead.

.card {
  margin-inline: 24px;       /* left + right (or right + left in RTL) */
  margin-block: 16px;        /* top + bottom */
  padding-inline-start: 12px; /* left in LTR, right in RTL */
  border-inline-end: 2px solid purple;
}

Write your CSS once and it works correctly in every writing direction.

7. accent-color

Theme checkboxes, radios, range sliders, and progress bars with one property:

:root { accent-color: #7c3aed; }

/* All form controls now use your brand color */
input[type="checkbox"] { /* ... */ }
input[type="radio"]    { /* ... */ }
input[type="range"]    { /* ... */ }
progress               { /* ... */ }

8. color-mix()

Blend two colors in CSS. Perfect for hover states and theme variants:

.btn {
  background: #7c3aed;
}
.btn:hover {
  /* 80% of the primary + 20% black = darker variant */
  background: color-mix(in oklab, #7c3aed 80%, black);
}

/* Or mix a brand color with the surface color */
.accent-bg {
  background: color-mix(in oklab, var(--brand), white 85%);
}

9. :focus-visible

Show focus rings only for keyboard users, not mouse clicks:

button:focus { outline: none; }
button:focus-visible {
  outline: 2px solid #06b6d4;
  outline-offset: 2px;
}

10. scroll-snap for Carousels

Build a carousel with pure CSS scrolling that 'snaps' to each card:

.carousel {
  display: flex;
  overflow-x: auto;
  scroll-snap-type: x mandatory;
  gap: 16px;
  padding: 16px;
}
.slide {
  flex: 0 0 80%;
  scroll-snap-align: center;
  aspect-ratio: 16/9;
}

No JavaScript library needed. The browser handles momentum scrolling and snap-to-center.

11. text-wrap: balance and pretty

h1, h2, h3 {
  text-wrap: balance;
  /* Distributes words evenly across lines */
}

p {
  text-wrap: pretty;
  /* Avoids orphans in long text */
}

Headings stop ending with a single word on the last line. Paragraphs stop leaving one word alone. Beautiful typography in two lines.

12. dvh, svh, lvh (Dynamic Viewport Units)

On mobile, the address bar appears and disappears, changing the viewport. 100vh breaks because it assumes the toolbar is hidden. Use the new units:

.hero {
  min-height: 100dvh; /* dynamic - adjusts as toolbars show/hide */
  /* svh = small (with toolbars), lvh = large (without) */
}

codeExamples.title: Container Query: Card That Adapts to Its Container
codeExamples.code:
Narrow column

Aarav's Blog Post

This column is narrow, so the card stacks vertically.

Wide column

Priya's Blog Post

Same card, same HTML, same CSS. But because this column is wider, the container query kicks in and the card becomes horizontal.
codeExamples.explanation: Each .column is marked as a container with container-type: inline-size. The .card inside has a default vertical layout, but when its container is at least 380px wide, a container query switches it to a horizontal layout. The SAME card, the SAME CSS — but it adapts to its container, not the viewport.
codeExamples.output: Two cards side by side. The narrow left card stacks the image above the text. The wider right card places the image beside the text, even though both use the exact same markup and CSS classes.

codeExamples.title: Fluid Typography with clamp()
codeExamples.code:
Fluid Typography Without a Single Media Query

Every font size and padding value on this page uses clamp(). Resize the browser window and watch everything scale smoothly - no breakpoints, no jumps. Ishita built her whole portfolio this way.

See How It Works
codeExamples.explanation: Every size on the page — padding, font size, margin — uses clamp(min, preferred, max). On a phone, values stay at the minimum. On a desktop, they reach the maximum. In between, they scale linearly with the viewport. Zero media queries. text-wrap: balance on the heading distributes the words evenly.
codeExamples.output: A beautiful hero with a large gradient heading, subtitle, and call-to-action button. Resizing the browser smoothly scales everything from phone to desktop sizes without any sudden jumps.

codeExamples.title: :has() to Disable Submit on Invalid Form
codeExamples.code: Rohan's Sign Up
codeExamples.explanation: :has() walks up from any invalid input to the form and styles the form (red border, disabled button, warning message). When every input is valid, form:has(input:valid):not(:has(input:invalid)) matches and the form turns green. Pure CSS reactive form state — something that used to need JavaScript.
codeExamples.output: A signup form that turns pink/red while any field is invalid and green once all fields are valid. The submit button is disabled while invalid, and a status message updates automatically.

codeExamples.title: CSS-Only Carousel with scroll-snap
codeExamples.code:
Vivaan's Projects

Quiz App
Weather Dashboard
To-Do List
Blog Engine
Calculator
codeExamples.explanation: A horizontal scrolling carousel made with scroll-snap-type: x mandatory on the container and scroll-snap-align: center on each slide. The browser handles smooth scrolling and snaps each slide to the center when the user stops dragging. No JavaScript, no libraries, no swipe gestures to write.
codeExamples.output: A horizontal carousel of 5 colorful gradient slides. Scrolling with the mouse wheel, touch, or trackpad smoothly snaps each slide to the center of the viewport.

codeExamples.title: aspect-ratio and color-mix() Color Palette
codeExamples.code:
Kavya's Generated Color Palette

Brand100%
Tint 80+ white 20
Tint 60+ white 40
Tint 40+ white 60
Tint 20+ white 80
Shade 80+ black 20
Shade 60+ black 40
Mix+ cyan
codeExamples.explanation: color-mix(in oklab, color1 X%, color2) blends two colors. By mixing the brand color with white and black in different ratios, we generate a full tint-and-shade palette without a design tool. Every swatch is a perfect square thanks to aspect-ratio: 1.
codeExamples.output: A grid of perfectly square color swatches generated entirely from a single brand color. Light tints in the top rows, darker shades below, and a blended accent color at the end.

codeExamples.title: Subgrid for Aligned Card Layout
codeExamples.code:
Anika's Blog Grid

Learning CSS Grid

My short take on why Grid changed everything for frontend work.

3 min read

Subgrid Finally Arrived

A deep dive on subgrid and why it fixes the last missing piece. This post has a longer description because I got excited while writing it.

8 min read

Container Queries in Practice

Rebuilding my card component without a single media query.

5 min read
codeExamples.explanation: The outer grid defines four rows shared by all cards. Each card uses grid-template-rows: subgrid so its children (image, title, paragraph, meta) snap to the parent grid's rows. The result: titles align across cards even when image positions or text lengths differ. This is the classic 'card alignment' problem finally solved in pure CSS.
codeExamples.output: Three blog cards with images, titles, descriptions, and meta info. All titles sit at the same vertical position, all meta lines line up at the bottom - even though the middle card has different content order and length.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting container-type Before Using @container
commonMistakes.error: The container query does nothing because no element has been marked as a container. The browser ignores the @container rule.
commonMistakes.explanation: Container queries require you to declare which element is the container using container-type. Without it, there is nothing for the query to match against.

commonMistakes.title: Using vh on Mobile Causing Layout Jumps
commonMistakes.error: On mobile, the address bar shows and hides as the user scrolls. 100vh does not account for this, causing layout shifts and cut-off content.
commonMistakes.explanation: Use 100dvh (dynamic viewport height) instead of 100vh for full-screen sections. It adjusts correctly as mobile browser toolbars show and hide. Keep 100vh as a fallback for old browsers.

commonMistakes.title: Expecting clamp() Arguments in Any Order
commonMistakes.error: The argument order is wrong. clamp(min, preferred, max) — first is the lower bound, second is the fluid value, third is the upper bound.
commonMistakes.explanation: Memorize: clamp(min, preferred, max). The preferred value is the one that scales with the viewport (like 5vw), and it is clamped between the min and max. Getting the order wrong often 'works' but at unexpected sizes.

commonMistakes.title: Using :has() Inside :has() Unnecessarily
commonMistakes.error: Nested :has() is slower and harder to read. In most cases a single :has() is enough.
commonMistakes.explanation: :has(input:invalid) already looks at all descendants. You rarely need to nest it. Nested :has() can be slower and is harder to read. Use the simplest form that matches your intent.

summary: Container queries (container-type: inline-size + @container) let you style a component based on its CONTAINER's size, not the viewport. This is the piece missing for true component-driven responsive design.

Subgrid (grid-template-rows: subgrid or grid-template-columns: subgrid) makes a nested grid's children align to the PARENT grid's tracks. Use it to align titles, images, and footers perfectly across a row of cards.
clamp(min, preferred, max) is the fluid value superpower. Use it for typography, padding, margins, and gap values to get smooth scaling between phone and desktop sizes without a single media query.
aspect-ratio: 16/9 locks an element's width-to-height ratio. No more padding-top hacks. No more layout shift when images load. Just set one dimension and the other follows.

:has() is the long-awaited CSS parent selector. It lets you style an element based on what it contains: .card:has(img), form:has(:invalid), label:has(:checked). Replaces huge amounts of JavaScript class-toggling.
Logical properties (margin-inline, padding-block, border-inline-start) use 'reading direction' instead of 'left/right', so your CSS works correctly in right-to-left languages too.

accent-color themes checkboxes, radios, range sliders, and progress bars in one property. color-mix(in oklab, color 80%, white) blends colors to generate tints, shades, and hover variants from a single brand color.
:focus-visible shows focus rings only when the user is navigating with the keyboard, keeping the UI clean for mouse users without breaking accessibility.
scroll-snap-type and scroll-snap-align build native carousels and galleries with zero JavaScript. The browser handles smooth scrolling and snap-to-center.
text-wrap: balance balances the lines of a heading so it does not end with an orphan word. text-wrap: pretty does the same for paragraphs. Beautiful typography in one property.
100dvh (dynamic viewport height) replaces 100vh on mobile, accounting for the address bar showing and hiding. Prevents layout jumps on scroll.

topicSpecific.question: What does aspect-ratio: 16/9 do?
topicSpecific.answer: Locks the element's width-to-height ratio at 16:9. If you set width to 320px, height becomes 180px automatically.
topicSpecific.explanation: aspect-ratio keeps a fixed ratio between width and height. Set one dimension and the other is calculated. No more padding-top hacks for videos, images, or cards.

topicSpecific.question: What does clamp(16px, 5vw, 32px) do?
topicSpecific.answer: Returns 5vw of the viewport width, but never less than 16px and never more than 32px. A smooth fluid value.
topicSpecific.explanation: clamp(min, preferred, max) gives you the preferred value bounded by the min and max. Perfect for fluid typography that stays legible at all sizes.

topicSpecific.question: What does :has() select?
topicSpecific.answer: An element that contains a descendant matching the inner selector. .card:has(img) matches a card that contains an image.
topicSpecific.explanation: :has() finally gives CSS a parent selector. It walks up from a matching descendant and matches the parent.

topicSpecific.question: Why is 100dvh better than 100vh on mobile?
topicSpecific.answer: On mobile, the address bar shows and hides as the user scrolls. 100dvh (dynamic viewport height) updates to reflect this. 100vh does not, causing layout jumps.
topicSpecific.explanation: dvh, svh, and lvh are the new viewport units. dvh follows the current viewport. svh is the small viewport (with toolbars showing). lvh is the large viewport (toolbars hidden).

topicSpecific.question: What does text-wrap: balance do on a heading?
topicSpecific.answer: It rewraps the heading so each line has roughly the same number of words, avoiding a lonely orphan word on the last line.
topicSpecific.explanation: text-wrap: balance is a typography win. Before it, headings often looked awkward because the last line had one word. Now the browser balances the wrapping for you.

topicSpecific.question: What is container-type: inline-size used for?
topicSpecific.answer: It marks the element as a query container so descendants can use @container queries based on its width.
topicSpecific.explanation: Without container-type, the browser does not track the element's size for container queries. inline-size is the most common value — it means 'only track the inline axis (width)'.

topicSpecific.question: What does :focus-visible match?
topicSpecific.answer: Focused elements, but only when the browser thinks the focus came from the keyboard (not from a mouse click).
topicSpecific.explanation: :focus-visible keeps focus rings invisible for mouse users but visible for keyboard users. It is the modern standard for accessible focus styling.

topicSpecific.question: What does margin-inline: 20px do?
topicSpecific.answer: Sets margin-left and margin-right each to 20px (in left-to-right languages). In right-to-left languages, it sets right and left accordingly.
topicSpecific.explanation: Logical properties use 'inline' (text direction) and 'block' (flow direction) instead of left/right/top/bottom. They work correctly in any writing direction.

topicSpecific.question: What does accent-color: #7c3aed style?
topicSpecific.answer: It sets the 'accent' color used to draw checkboxes, radio buttons, range sliders, and progress bars. All native form controls become purple.
topicSpecific.explanation: accent-color was introduced to give developers a one-line way to theme form controls that historically were very hard to style.

topicSpecific.question: Why use container queries instead of media queries?
topicSpecific.hint: Components do not know about viewports.
topicSpecific.answer: A component does not know how wide the viewport is — it knows how wide its container is. Container queries let the same component adapt correctly whether it is in a narrow sidebar or a wide hero.
topicSpecific.explanation: Media queries are based on the viewport. Container queries are based on the container of a component. This is the last missing piece for truly reusable, responsive components.

topicSpecific.answer: The card becomes a grid whose rows are inherited from its parent grid. grid-row: span 3 makes it occupy 3 parent rows, and its children snap to those parent tracks.
topicSpecific.explanation: subgrid is the magic that lets nested grid items align to the parent grid. Use it to align titles and footers across a row of cards of different heights.

topicSpecific.question: What does color-mix(in oklab, #7c3aed 80%, white) produce?
topicSpecific.answer: A light tint of purple (80% brand purple + 20% white). It is a pastel version of the original color.
topicSpecific.explanation: color-mix() blends two colors in a chosen color space. oklab gives perceptually-uniform mixing (no ugly muddy middles). Great for generating tints, shades, and hover variants from a base color.

topicSpecific.answer: When any descendant of the body is a modal with the open attribute, the body's scrolling is disabled. Great for locking the background when a modal is visible.
topicSpecific.explanation: :has() makes this one line. Without it, you had to toggle a class on the body from JavaScript whenever the modal opened or closed.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between svh, lvh, and dvh?
topicSpecific.answer: svh: small viewport (with toolbars showing). lvh: large viewport (with toolbars hidden). dvh: dynamic - updates live as toolbars show/hide.
topicSpecific.explanation: All three were added to solve the mobile address bar problem. dvh is what you usually want: a value that tracks the current viewport.

topicSpecific.answer: A horizontal scrolling carousel where each slide snaps to the center of the viewport as the user scrolls. No JavaScript.
topicSpecific.explanation: scroll-snap-type: x mandatory tells the browser to snap to points along the x-axis. scroll-snap-align: center aligns each slide to the center of the scroll container.

topicSpecific.question: What does padding-block-start: 16px do in a left-to-right language?
topicSpecific.answer: It sets padding-top: 16px. In LTR languages, the 'block' axis is vertical and 'start' is the top.
topicSpecific.explanation: Logical properties map differently depending on writing direction. In LTR horizontal text: inline = left/right, block = top/bottom. In Arabic (RTL): inline is reversed. In vertical-rl (like Japanese top-to-bottom), block is horizontal.

topicSpecific.answer: No. Without container-type: inline-size (or size) on .sidebar, the browser does not track its size and the @container rule never matches.
topicSpecific.explanation: Container queries require an explicit container declaration. The @container at-rule without a named container walks up to the nearest ancestor that has a container-type set.

topicSpecific.question: What does font-size: clamp(1rem, 3vw + 0.5rem, 2rem) do?
topicSpecific.answer: Font size is 3vw plus 0.5rem, but clamped between 1rem and 2rem. This is a common pattern for smooth but bounded fluid typography.
topicSpecific.explanation: The preferred argument in clamp() can be any expression, including sums of different units. Adding a fixed rem to a viewport unit gives a gentler slope than pure vw.

topicSpecific.question: What selects labels whose input is checked?
topicSpecific.answer: label:has(input:checked)
topicSpecific.explanation: :has() matches a label containing a checked input. Before :has(), you would have to use the general sibling combinator and carefully structure your HTML.

topicSpecific.question:
What is wrong with this?
h1 { font-size: clamp(5vw, 32px, 60px); }
topicSpecific.answer: The arguments are in the wrong order. It should be clamp(min, preferred, max), so: clamp(32px, 5vw, 60px).
topicSpecific.explanation: The first argument is the minimum, the second is the preferred (fluid) value, and the third is the maximum. Getting the order wrong may still 'work' but at unexpected values.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this do?
.thumbnail { aspect-ratio: 1; width: 80px; }
topicSpecific.answer: Creates an 80x80 square. The width is 80px and aspect-ratio: 1 makes the height match.
topicSpecific.explanation: aspect-ratio: 1 is shorthand for 1 / 1 — a square. Set width (or height) and the other dimension follows automatically.

topicSpecific.question:
Is this valid?
input[type="range"] { accent-color: #7c3aed; }
topicSpecific.answer: Yes. accent-color styles the track and thumb of range sliders with the specified color.
topicSpecific.explanation: accent-color works on <input type="checkbox">, <input type="radio">, <input type="range">, and <progress>.

topicSpecific.question: Why is subgrid so useful for card grids?
topicSpecific.answer: Without subgrid, each card has its own grid and the internal rows of different cards do not align. With subgrid, cards share the parent's row tracks, so all titles, bodies, and footers line up across the whole row.
topicSpecific.explanation: The classic 'uneven card content' problem is solved: even if one card has a longer title or a bigger image, the 'Read More' button at the bottom of every card still lines up perfectly.

topicSpecific.question: Name three things :has() replaces that used to require JavaScript.
topicSpecific.answer: 1) Toggling a class on a parent based on child state. 2) Disabling a submit button while any input is invalid. 3) Hiding body scroll when a modal is open. 4) Highlighting a card that contains an image. And many more.
topicSpecific.explanation: Most 'reactive CSS' patterns that needed JavaScript event listeners can now be written as a single CSS selector with :has(). The saving in code and complexity is enormous.

topicSpecific.answer: When the nearest ancestor with container-name: card (and a container-type) is at least 500px wide, .title gets a 28px font size.
topicSpecific.explanation: @container name (query) matches only the container with that name. This lets you have multiple levels of containers and target specific ones.

topicSpecific.question:
What is wrong?
body:has(form:has(input:invalid)) { background: red; }
topicSpecific.answer: Nested :has() is usually unnecessary. Use body:has(input:invalid) instead. :has() already walks the whole subtree.
topicSpecific.explanation: Nested :has() is valid but slower and harder to read. Unless you need to match two different relationships, flatten to a single :has().

topicSpecific.question:
What happens here?
:root { accent-color: magenta; }
.dark { accent-color: cyan; }
topicSpecific.answer: Form controls use magenta by default but cyan inside any element with the dark class. accent-color is inherited.
topicSpecific.explanation: accent-color inherits down the DOM tree, so you can theme different sections of a page differently just by setting it on a parent element.

topicSpecific.hint: Parent selector with a descendant combinator.
topicSpecific.answer: Yes. It selects h3 elements inside a card that contains a .featured descendant. The red color applies only to cards marked as featured.
topicSpecific.explanation: :has() can be combined with descendant combinators. Here, we first pick cards that contain a .featured element, then style h3 descendants of those cards.

topicSpecific.question: What does interpolate-size: allow-keywords do?
topicSpecific.answer: It lets CSS transition from a fixed size to auto (or min-content, max-content, etc.), which historically was impossible. Enables animating accordions open without JavaScript.
topicSpecific.explanation: Before interpolate-size, animating from 0 to height: auto did not work — browsers could not interpolate. The new property unlocks long-awaited accordion and dropdown animations.

topicSpecific.question: When would you use container-type: size instead of inline-size?
topicSpecific.answer: Use size when you need to query the container's height as well. It tracks both width and height. Use inline-size (more efficient) when you only need width, which is most of the time.
topicSpecific.explanation: size is more expensive because the browser must track both dimensions. Use it only when your design actually depends on container height — for example, a card that changes layout based on available vertical space.

topicSpecific.question: Why is color-mix(in oklab, ...) preferred over in srgb?
topicSpecific.answer: oklab is a perceptually-uniform color space, so mixing produces visually smooth transitions without the muddy greens and browns you sometimes get in sRGB. Mixing red and blue in oklab gives a clean purple.
topicSpecific.explanation: sRGB mixing can produce unexpected intermediate colors because the color space is not perceptually uniform. oklab (and oklch) were designed for uniform perception, so mixing, lightening, and darkening produce more natural results.

topicSpecific.question: How does text-wrap: balance work differently from manual <br> tags?
topicSpecific.answer: Manual line breaks force a break at a fixed position regardless of screen size, often breaking badly on narrow or wide screens. text-wrap: balance lets the browser rebalance at any size, so the heading always looks well-wrapped.
topicSpecific.explanation: Balance is responsive — it recalculates on every resize. Manual <br> is a static commitment to a specific break position. Use balance for headings, let the browser do the work.

mcqs.question: What does aspect-ratio: 16/9 do?

options.A: Sets the width to 16px and height to 9px
options.B: Locks the ratio of width to height at 16:9

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. aspect-ratio locks the width-to-height ratio. Set one dimension and the other is calculated automatically.

mcqs.question: Which is the correct order for clamp()?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. clamp(min, preferred, max). The preferred value is used, but never below the min or above the max.

mcqs.question: What does :has() enable that CSS historically could not do?

options.B: Select a parent based on its children

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. :has() is the parent selector. It lets you match an element based on what it contains, which CSS could not do before 2022-2023.

mcqs.question: What must an element have to participate in container queries?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Without container-type, the browser does not track the element's size for container queries.

mcqs.question: Which unit should you prefer over 100vh on mobile?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. 100dvh (dynamic viewport height) adjusts as mobile browser toolbars show and hide, preventing layout jumps.

mcqs.question: What does text-wrap: balance do?

options.B: Distributes words across lines to avoid orphans

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. It rebalances wrapping so a heading does not end with a single orphan word. Great for hero titles.

mcqs.question: Which property themes all native form controls in one line?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. accent-color sets the theme color for checkboxes, radios, range sliders, and progress bars.

mcqs.question: What is margin-inline: 20px in a left-to-right language?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. In LTR languages, the inline axis is horizontal. margin-inline sets left and right margin. In RTL languages it still sets the start-and-end direction correctly.

mcqs.question: What does container-type: inline-size mean?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. inline-size tracks the container's inline (width) dimension. It is more efficient than size and is what you want most of the time.

mcqs.question: What is subgrid used for?

options.B: Making a nested grid share its parent's tracks

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. grid-template-columns: subgrid (or rows) makes a child grid use the parent's tracks, aligning descendants across siblings.

mcqs.question: Which :focus-visible statement is TRUE?

options.A: It only matches on mouse clicks
options.B: It matches when the browser decides the focus is keyboard-like

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. :focus-visible is the modern focus ring pseudo-class. It shows the ring for keyboard users but hides it for mouse users who do not need it.

mcqs.question: How do you disable a submit button while any input is invalid, with just CSS?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. :has() checks if the form contains an invalid input. If so, the rule disables and fades the button.

mcqs.question: What does color-mix(in oklab, red 50%, blue) produce?

options.A: A 50/50 blend of red and blue in oklab color space (a clean purple)

mcqs.explanation: A is correct. color-mix() blends two colors in the specified color space. oklab gives perceptually-uniform mixing.

mcqs.question: Which is the right way to build a carousel with pure CSS?

options.B: Use scroll-snap-type on the container and scroll-snap-align on each slide

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. scroll-snap gives you native carousel scrolling that snaps to each slide, with no JavaScript required.

mcqs.question: Which viewport unit updates live as mobile toolbars show/hide?
mcqs.explanation: D is correct. dvh (dynamic viewport height) updates continuously to match the current visible viewport.

mcqs.question: You have @container (min-width: 400px) { ... } with NO container-type set on any ancestor. What happens?

options.A: It falls back to media query
options.B: It never matches — nothing has been declared as a container

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Without container-type on any ancestor, the query has no container to match and never activates.

mcqs.question: Which is the best fluid font-size declaration?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. clamp() gives a fluid value bounded by a readable minimum and a reasonable maximum. Pure vw becomes too small on mobile and too large on desktop.

mcqs.question:
What does this do?
label:has(input:checked) { background: #7c3aed; color: white; }

options.B: Styles only labels that contain a currently-checked input

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. :has(input:checked) matches labels with a checked input inside. Clicking the label ticks the input, which instantly triggers the highlight style.

codingChallenges.title: Fluid Typography Hero
codingChallenges.description: Build a full-screen hero section with a large heading, subtitle, and CTA button. Use clamp() for every font-size and padding value so the whole section scales smoothly from mobile to desktop. Use text-wrap: balance on the heading. Use 100dvh for the height. Name the project after Aarav.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A hero that scales smoothly from phone to desktop without any media queries.
codingChallenges.constraints: No media queries. Use clamp() for fluid values. Use 100dvh. Use text-wrap: balance.
codingChallenges.solution:
Aarav's Frontend Universe

Every size on this page uses clamp(). No breakpoints. No media queries. Pure fluid design that looks perfect at any viewport size.

Explore Projects

codingChallenges.title: Square Avatar Grid with aspect-ratio
codingChallenges.description: Build a responsive grid of 8 user avatars for Priya, Rohan, Ishita, Vivaan, Anika, Kavya, Meera, and Ishaan. Every avatar MUST be a perfect square thanks to aspect-ratio: 1. Use a responsive grid with repeat(auto-fit, minmax()). Each avatar is a gradient with the student's initial.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A responsive grid of 8 square gradient avatars, each showing the user's initial.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use aspect-ratio. Use auto-fit minmax. No fixed height on avatars.
codingChallenges.solution:

codingChallenges.title: Card That Responds to Its Container
codingChallenges.description: Build a card component that uses container queries. Place the same card in two containers: a narrow column and a wide column. When the container is wider than 380px, the card should switch from vertical (image above text) to horizontal (image beside text). Use container-type: inline-size and @container.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Two copies of the same card component with different layouts based on their container widths.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use container queries. No media queries. Same CSS class for both cards.
codingChallenges.solution:
Narrow column

Meera's Blog Post

This column is narrow, so the same card stacks vertically with the image on top.

Wide column

Ishaan's Blog Post

Same card, same HTML, same CSS class. But the container query detects a wider parent and switches to a horizontal layout automatically.

codingChallenges.title: Pure CSS Carousel with scroll-snap
codingChallenges.description: Build a horizontal carousel with 6 project cards (name them after Aarav, Priya, Rohan, Ishita, Vivaan, and Anika). Use scroll-snap-type: x mandatory on the container and scroll-snap-align: center on each slide. Each slide should be a gradient with the project name. No JavaScript.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A smooth scrolling carousel that snaps each card to the center.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use scroll-snap. No JavaScript. Use aspect-ratio on slides.
codingChallenges.solution:
Student Showcase

Aarav's Quiz App
Priya's Weather
Rohan's Calculator
Ishita's Blog
Vivaan's Portfolio
Anika's Timer

codingChallenges.title: Subgrid Card Row with Aligned Content
codingChallenges.description: Build a row of 3 blog cards where the title, body, and 'Read More' link align perfectly across all cards, even though the content lengths differ. Use grid-template-rows: subgrid on each card. The parent grid must define the row structure.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Three cards where each title starts at the same y-position and each 'Read More' sits at the same y-position, regardless of body length.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use subgrid. Do not use fixed heights on the cards.
codingChallenges.solution:

codingChallenges.title: Reactive Form with :has() and color-mix()
codingChallenges.description: Build a signup form that changes color state using :has(). When any input is invalid, the form border is red and the submit button is disabled. When all are valid, the border turns green. Use color-mix() to generate hover and disabled color variants from a single brand color. No JavaScript.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: The form reacts to validation state in real time: red border + disabled button when invalid, green border + enabled button when all valid.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use :has() for the parent reaction. Use color-mix() for variants. No JavaScript.
codingChallenges.solution: Ishaan's Signup Form
